danger_signals.visual_form: Loud squealing noise auditory cue; visual heat indicator or smoke effects (inferred from mechanical failure state)
danger_signals.meaning: Rapid downshifting or over-revving causes engine damage; player must manage throttle to avoid forced retirement

danger_signals.visual_form: Tire hardness degradation tracking; worn tires require pit stop replacement
danger_signals.meaning: Continued racing without pit stop maintenance leads to forced retirement; visual tire condition deterioration (inferred)

danger_signals.visual_form: Signs, adboards, and platforms positioned close to track edges; visual impact feedback
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazards increase difficulty; contact causes damage based on difficulty level (damage tolerance decreases at higher difficulties)

danger_signals.visual_form: Difficulty-dependent car damage accumulation; visual damage model or performance degradation
danger_signals.meaning: Higher difficulty levels reduce damage tolerance; accumulated damage from collisions forces retirement if threshold exceeded

safety_signals.visual_form: Pit lane location marked on track; pit crew station visualization
safety_signals.meaning: Player can safely service tires and manage car setup; mandatory pit stop prevents forced retirement

safety_signals.visual_form: Clear racing line without obstacles; straightaways and wider track sections
safety_signals.meaning: Safe driving zones without collision hazards; player can maintain speed safely

safety_signals.visual_form: RPM gauge feedback; absence of squealing audio cue
safety_signals.meaning: Controlled gear usage maintains engine health; careful throttle management prevents overheat

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Driver models: Riccardo Patrese and Michael Schumacher with Benetton-branded car

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Ferrari prancing horse; Ferrari team livery
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Driver models: Jean Alesi and Gerhard Berger with Ferrari-branded car
Driver models: Michael Andretti and Mika Häkkinen with McLaren-branded car
Driver models: Damon Hill and Alain Prost with Williams-branded car
Driver models: Mark Blundell and Martin Brundle with Ligier-branded car
Driver models: Johnny Herbert and Alessandro Zanardi with Lotus-branded car
Driver models: Andrea de Cesaris and Ukyo Katayama with Tyrrell-branded car
Player-controlled car with Domark branding; driver: James Tripp
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Pit crew station at Silverstone shows 'retired' cars as environmental storytelling

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 12 tracks representing 1993 F1 calendar (Interlagos, Imola, Barcelona, Monaco, Montreal, Castelet, Silverstone, Hockenheim, Spa, Monza, Estoril, Adelaide); correct race order; licensed drivers and teams with authentic liveries
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Competitor AI drivers behave as their real-world counterparts would; dynamic difficulty scaling mimics competitive racing; pit stop requirements reflect 1993 tire technology constraints

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Setup menu displays before race start; tire hardness, wing angle, transmission configuration screens
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must configure car before competing; setup choices affect handling and performance during race

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Split-screen for multiplayer mode; turbo mode reduces sprite/polygon detail to increase perceived speed; track obstacles and adboards position hazards strategically
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Competitors overtake/are overtaken; damage accumulates from collisions; engine temperature increases with poor throttle management; tire wear forces pit stop necessity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Setup menu reappears; pit stop service screen displays; championship standings update
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can adjust strategy for next race; mandatory pit stops during race prevent continued racing with worn tires

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Identical track layouts but cosmetic damage indicators may vary; obstacle placement constant
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Competitor speed increases with difficulty; player car damage tolerance decreases; Expert mode creates higher collision risk and engine failure likelihood

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Loud squealing audio cue signals overheat; visual engine damage or smoke effects (inferred from mechanical failure)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Over-revving from rapid downshifting or collision-induced jumping causes engine stress; forced retirement if damage threshold exceeded

environmental_storytelling.example: Signs, adboards, and platforms positioned near track edges; Silverstone features two 'retired' cars (one at pits, one mid-track) serving as visual metaphor for competition consequence and mechanical failure
1993-accurate track designs from real F1 venues; track geometry communicates historical racing geography and venue authenticity

Each of 8 teams (Benetton, Ferrari, McLaren, Williams, Ligier, Lotus, Tyrrell, Domark) displays distinct car colors and sponsor logos; visual identification allows player to recognize competitors at a glance
Pit crew station positioned on track map; visual location communicates service zone availability and mandatory pit stop requirement for tire replacement
Squealing tire noise from over-revving communicates mechanical stress; auditory signal warns player of imminent engine failure without explicit HUD warning

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Maintains position relative to player; overtakes when opportunity arises; defensive positioning on straights and curves
Represents competitive Benetton team threat; higher difficulty tiers amplify their speed and aggression
Responsive to throttle, braking, and steering input; smooth handling physics allow high-speed maneuvers
Loud squealing noise triggers; RPM gauge spikes; continued abuse forces engine failure and retirement; collision knockback amplifies over-rev risk
Handling degrades; pit stop becomes mandatory; continued racing without tire change forces retirement
Performance degrades with collision damage; at Expert difficulty, threshold to forced retirement becomes very low; visual damage model (inferred) deteriorates
Static hazards increase difficulty; collision causes damage; strategic placement near corners and straights creates risk zones
Await player pit stop for tire service; mandatory stop required to continue racing; crew visualization at Silverstone includes two retired cars as environmental context

notes: F1 World Championship Edition (1993) primarily communicates world-state through authentic team liveries, licensed drivers, and accurate 1993 F1 calendar. Danger signals rely heavily on mechanical failure feedback (audio cues for overheat, tire wear mechanics). Faction identity is purely visual through team car colors and driver rosters—no competitive 'factions' in narrative sense, but racing teams serve as gameplay-relevant entities. Environmental storytelling is minimal but effective: retired cars at Silverstone hint at mechanical failure consequences. The game uses arcade abstraction rather than simulation, so visual manifestations of damage/wear are inferred from gameplay mechanics rather than explicitly documented. The 'Turbo Mode' visual reduction and 'split-screen multiplayer' sprite shrinking are intentional manifestations of hardware speed optimization, not world-state communication.
